title: Move DIV/REM off ALU critical path
motivation:
The current ALU synthesizes signed and unsigned divide/remainder operators in
the same combinational block as the add, shift, branch, and multiply paths.
Even when CoreMark's timed loop rarely or never retires DIV/REM, that logic can
dominate FPGA Fmax and consume LUTs on the Tang Nano 20K. Moving rare division
work out of the single-cycle ALU should improve the Fmax term of the fitness
product with little CoreMark IPC cost.

Replace the real-hardware DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU datapaths with a compact iterative
divider/remainder unit that only stalls EX while one of those opcodes is active.
Keep MUL/MULH single-cycle for CoreMark's multiply-heavy matrix work, keep the
RISCV_FORMAL_ALTOPS substitutes combinational under `RISCV_FORMAL_ALTOPS`, and
allow RVFI retirement only after the multi-cycle result is available. Because
DIV/REM are rare or absent in the CoreMark timed region, expected iter/cycle
loss is near zero while Fmax and LUT pressure improve.

title: Iterative DIV/REM execution unit
motivation:
The current EX stage puts RV32M division and remainder in the combinational
ALU. Even if CoreMark rarely executes DIV/REM in the timed region, static
timing and LUT mapping still carry the divider network through the ALU result
mux, keeping LUT usage near the 10k budget and likely limiting Fmax. CoreMark's
hot path mostly needs adds, branches, loads, stores, and multiply-family ops,
so moving only DIV/REM off the single-cycle path should buy clock rate with
little timed-cycle cost.

title: Static loop branch predictor
motivation:
The current five-stage core resolves all branches and jumps in EX and flushes
the two younger instructions on every taken control transfer. CoreMark spends
substantial time in tight counted loops, so backward branches and direct JALs
repeatedly pay this fixed penalty even when their target is available from the
fetched instruction bits. Avoiding correct taken-loop flushes should improve
CoreMark iterations per cycle with modest LUT cost and limited Fmax risk.

fetched instruction bits. Avoiding correct taken-loop flushes should improve

Add a small static predictor in IF: predict direct JAL taken and predict
conditional branches taken only when their B-immediate is negative, while all
forward branches and JALR remain predicted not-taken. Carry the predicted
next PC through ID/EX, then in EX compute the resolved next PC and assert the
existing redirect/flush path only when the prediction was wrong. Correctly
predicted backward loop branches will continue fetching from the loop target
instead of flushing, while mispredictions still retire with precise RVFI
pc_wdata and recover by redirecting to the resolved PC.

title: Move high-half multiply off ALU path
motivation:
The current best result came from removing rare DIV/REM logic from the
single-cycle ALU critical path. The remaining ALU still builds three 64-bit
multiplier products every cycle so MUL, MULH, MULHU, and MULHSU can all be
selected combinationally. CoreMark needs fast low-half MUL for matrix work,
but high-half multiply opcodes are expected to be cold or absent in the timed
region, so their multiplier trees are likely paying Fmax and routing cost
without improving CoreMark iterations per cycle.

but high-half multiply opcodes are expected to be cold or absent in the timed

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Keep ALU_MUL as the only real single-cycle multiply in the fast ALU path and
move MULH/MULHU/MULHSU into a small multi-cycle high-half multiply unit that
stalls EX only for those opcodes. In the non-ALTOPS build, alu.sv should no
longer instantiate signed-signed and signed-unsigned 64-bit product trees on
every operation; ex_stage.sv should capture and retire the high-half result
through the existing EX stall/backpressure pattern used by the divider. Under
RISCV_FORMAL_ALTOPS, all M-extension substitute formulas remain
combinational so the existing formal configuration is unchanged. This should
reduce ALU mux fan-in, multiplier routing pressure, and worst-case Fmax cost
while preserving single-cycle MUL for the CoreMark hot path.

title: Move high multiply off fast ALU
motivation:
The current best already removes DIV/REM from the single-cycle ALU, but the

so it can return MULH, MULHU, and MULHSU in one EX cycle. CoreMark's hot path
benefits from keeping ordinary MUL fast, while high-half multiply operations
are rare in compiler-generated CoreMark code and still tax the FPGA timing
graph and LUT/DSP mapping every cycle.

title: Tiny dynamic BTB predictor
motivation:
The prior static loop predictor attacked the right CPI problem but put
immediate decode and branch-target adders on the fetch PC path, reducing Fmax
enough to lose overall fitness. CoreMark still pays repeated two-cycle flushes
on tight-loop taken branches and direct jumps, so the best predictor angle is
to learn already-resolved targets instead of recomputing them in IF.

hypothesis.motivation:

to learn already-resolved targets instead of recomputing them in IF.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a tiny direct-mapped BTB/BHT that predicts taken only on a tagged hit with
a taken-biased 2-bit counter and supplies a stored resolved next PC. The IF
stage then only performs a small table read, tag compare, and target mux;
branch immediates and target adders stay out of the fetch critical path.
Update the table in EX for resolved branches, JAL, and JALR, and redirect only
when the stored prediction disagrees with the resolved next PC. This should
recover most steady-state loop and call/return control-transfer bubbles while
avoiding the Fmax regression mode seen in the static IF predictor.

title: Qualify load-use hazards by source use
motivation:
The current hazard unit treats instr[19:15] and instr[24:20] as live
sources for every fetched instruction. For OP-IMM, LOAD, JALR,
LUI/AUIPC/JAL, FENCE, and EBREAK, one or both fields are immediate,
destination, or reserved bits, so a preceding load whose rd happens to
match those bits creates a false one-cycle bubble. CoreMark's load-heavy
compiled code likely contains many immediate address/math operations, and
with the current high-Fmax core a few unnecessary bubbles translate directly
into fitness loss.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a source-use qualifier for the IF/ID instruction and gate the existing
load-use compare with uses_rs1 and uses_rs2. R-type, branch, and store
instructions continue checking both operands; OP-IMM, LOAD, and JALR check
only rs1; LUI, AUIPC, JAL, FENCE, and EBREAK check neither. This preserves
all true load-use interlocks and forwarding behavior while removing
accidental stalls without touching the ALU, memory datapath, or RVFI
retirement paths. The added opcode decode is small and off the arithmetic
data path, so the expected gain is IPC rather than Fmax.

title: MEM-stage load-use bypass
motivation:
The current five-stage pipeline pays a fixed one-cycle bubble for every true
load-use pair, even when the dmem access is zero-wait or happens to be ready
in the random-stall CoreMark model. CoreMark's compiled hot loops are load
heavy, so this interlock directly reduces iterations per cycle. The existing
Fmax headroom from moving DIV/REM off the ALU path makes a carefully scoped
bypass from MEM to EX worth testing as a structural IPC improvement.

title: Return-stack JALR predictor
motivation:
CoreMark still pays the existing EX-stage redirect penalty for every taken
control transfer, but the prior predictor attempts show that general branch
prediction is dangerous on this FPGA when target adders, table reads, tag
compares, or dynamic target muxing sit on the fetch PC path. Function returns
are a narrower predictor target: compiler-generated RV32 code commonly emits
JALR x0, x1, 0 returns, and their target can be supplied from a small return
address stack without recomputing an immediate target in IF.

title: Share multiply product for MULH
motivation:
The current ALU still computes separate signed-signed, unsigned-unsigned, and
signed-unsigned 64-bit multiply products every cycle. Prior attempts to move
high-half multiply into a side unit regressed, so the better micro-opt target
is to keep all RV32M multiply operations single-cycle while removing redundant
multiplier hardware from the static timing graph.

hypothesis.motivation:
The current ALU still computes separate signed-signed, unsigned-unsigned, and
signed-unsigned 64-bit multiply products every cycle. Prior attempts to move
high-half multiply into a side unit regressed, so the better micro-opt target
is to keep all RV32M multiply operations single-cycle while removing redundant
multiplier hardware from the static timing graph.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Compute one unsigned 32x32 product in the real ALU path, use its low half for
MUL and MULHU, and derive MULH/MULHSU high halves with the standard signed
correction terms: subtract b from the high half when a is signed-negative, and
subtract a when b is signed-negative for signed-signed MULH. This should let
synthesis map a single multiplier plus small subtractors instead of three
independent product trees, improving LUT/routing pressure and Fmax without
adding stalls or changing CoreMark IPC. Leave RISCV_FORMAL_ALTOPS formulas
unchanged and validate edge cases through the existing ALU test coverage.

title: Decoupled fetch skid buffer
motivation:
The current front end has no instruction buffering: any imemReady=0 cycle
becomes an immediate decode bubble, and any load-use, dmem, or divider stall
also freezes the PC so the core cannot prefetch useful sequential work while
the back end is waiting. CoreMark is evaluated with roughly 22% random
instruction and data stalls, so overlapping fetch with already-existing
backend bubbles should improve iterations per cycle without revisiting the
failed BTB/static-branch predictor paths.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a tiny two-entry fetch/decode skid buffer inside the IF structure. When
ID/EX cannot consume because of a load-use bubble, dmem hold, or divider
hold, IF should continue advancing along the already-selected sequential or
RAS-predicted path when imemReady is high and enqueue the fetched instruction
plus its PC and prediction metadata. When imemReady is low but the buffer has
an entry, ID can consume the queued instruction instead of injecting a NOP.
Redirects keep priority, flush the buffer, and restart fetch at the resolved
target, so RVFI order and precise traps remain unchanged. This is a structural
latency-hiding change: it adds no BTB/BHT lookup, no branch immediate target
adder, and no new retirement channel, preserving the current Fmax-friendly
fetch critical path while reducing visible random iStall bubbles.

motivation: The current register file resets all 32x32 data flops, which prevents distributed RAM inference and leaves roughly a thousand synchronous-reset flip-flops on routing and reset networks. A storage-only register-file restructure is a narrower structural angle with no intended CoreMark cycle penalty.

title: One-entry store buffer
motivation:
The current pipeline freezes from MEM backward whenever a store reaches
EX/MEM during a dmemReady=0 cycle. In the CoreMark fitness run, dmem has
roughly 22% random stalls, and store stalls are easier to hide than load
stalls because stores do not need returned data before the producer retires.
Recent structural regressions mostly came from adding logic to the fetch PC
path, new broad bypass paths, or a register-file rewrite, so a narrow MEM-side
decoupling buffer is a better structural target.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a single-entry non-speculative store buffer in the MEM stage for normal
RAM stores only. When a valid, aligned store to the data-memory window reaches
MEM while dmemReady is low and the buffer is empty, capture its aligned
address, replicated write data, byte mask, and RVFI memory fields, let the
store advance to MEM/WB and retire once enqueued, then keep driving the
buffered write on the dmem port until the bus accepts it. MMIO stores to the
UART/CoreMark marker window must keep the current blocking behavior so timing
brackets and UART output remain cycle-accurate. Loads and younger stores must
stall while the buffer is occupied so in-order memory visibility is preserved;
younger ALU/control instructions can continue flowing because they do not
consume the dmem port. This should convert many random RAM-store wait cycles
into useful backend work without adding IF prediction, widening retirement, or
touching the MUL/DIV datapaths.

title: Speculative RAS pop for return chains
motivation:
The only successful predictor so far is the narrow return-address stack, while
every branch/BTB/loop predictor that put tags, target muxes, or target adders
near IF lost more Fmax than it recovered in CPI. The current RAS still waits
until EX to pop a predicted return, so a nested or back-to-back return fetched
before that EX update can see the stale top entry and mispredict even though
the next return address is already present deeper in the stack.

title: Prune post-EX control payload
motivation:
Recent micro-opt attempts show that this core is Fmax-sensitive and that adding
new front-end prediction, buffering, or side-unit control often costs more than
it saves. The current pipeline still carries the full decode control word and
dead branch metadata through EX/MEM and MEM/WB even though most of those bits
are only needed inside EX. Trimming this payload should reduce flip-flops,
routing, mux fanout, and control-set pressure without changing CoreMark IPC.

title: Registered IF/ID retiming boundary
motivation:
The current front end has no real IF/ID register: the PC drives imem, the
returned instruction is decoded, register-file addresses are selected, and
the ID/EX bundle is captured in the same cycle. After the successful divider
split, this fetch/decode/register-read cone is a plausible remaining Fmax
limiter, while recent structural attempts show that adding queues, predictor
muxes, store buffers, or broad bypass paths can cost more timing than they
recover.

A single retiming register is a narrower structural experiment. It targets
clock rate rather than adding new speculation or memory decoupling, and it
should preserve the current one-instruction-per-cycle steady-state issue rate.

branch prediction. Direct JAL is attractive because its target is exact,
requires no table state or tag compare, and complements the existing RAS by
removing the call-side redirect penalty.

title: Drop dead EX/MEM branch payload

even though no downstream module reads them, because RVFI already uses
`pc_next` for the resolved next PC. Removing only those dead registered fields
should slightly reduce flip-flops, assignment fanout, and routing pressure
without changing CoreMark iterations per cycle.

policy but reduce the IF-visible RAS top path.

title: Canonical x1 RAS discipline
motivation:
The only predictor that has improved this core is the exact canonical-return
RAS. Later predictor attempts regressed when they added fetch-path target
logic, broader return matches, speculative pops, or aggressive stack repair.

either x1 or x5 as the link register, but IF only predicts and EX only pops
the exact `JALR x0,x1,0` return form. Alternate-link x5 pushes can therefore
pollute the top entry for the x1-only predictor without creating a matching
predicted return opportunity.

title: Direct-arm decoder control

then runs a second case to choose controls, which can leave unnecessary compare
and mux fanout on this hot front-end path.

title: Late store-data load bypass
motivation:
CoreMark is load/store heavy, and the current five-stage pipeline stalls on
every load-use match against either raw IF/ID source field. For a following
store, the rs2 value is not needed in EX to compute the store address; it is
only needed one stage later when MEM drives dmem_wdata. That makes
load-to-store-data dependencies a narrow structural bubble that can be
removed without adding a general MEM-to-EX load bypass, a fetch buffer, or a
store queue, all of which have already shown high timing risk in prior
structural attempts.

title: Rollback-safe RAS lookahead
motivation:
The only predictor that has improved this core is the exact canonical-return
RAS, and the failed BTB/BHT, loop, direct-JAL, masked-return, and broad RAS
update attempts all point away from adding target adders, tags, or wider
instruction matches to IF. One real gap remains in the winning predictor:
the RAS is only popped when the return reaches EX, so back-to-back or nested
return chains can keep seeing the stale top entry for the younger return.

A prior speculative-pop attempt attacked this gap but regressed because IF
mutated predictor state without enough rollback discipline. The more promising
version is to make only the IF-visible read pointer speculative while keeping
the committed stack contents and architectural pop/push updates in EX.

The only predictor that has improved this core is the exact canonical-return
RAS, and the failed BTB/BHT, loop, direct-JAL, masked-return, and broad RAS
update attempts all point away from adding target adders, tags, or wider

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Keep the IF predictor policy exactly as narrow as the successful design:
predict only exact `JALR x0,x1,0` from the current instruction word and select
only a RAS-supplied target. Add a small speculative-pop debt counter inside
the return stack. When IF actually consumes a predicted canonical return, the
counter advances so the next IF lookup reads the next-deeper committed stack
entry; the stack array and committed depth are not changed until EX resolves
the return.

When that predicted return reaches EX, the normal committed pop consumes one
real entry and cancels the corresponding speculative debt. Any EX redirect or
return-target mismatch clears younger speculative debt back to the committed
state, so wrong-path return fetches cannot poison later predictions. This
should recover return-chain bubbles that the current EX-only pop misses, while
avoiding the failed speculative-RAS corruption mode and avoiding every broad
branch predictor structure that previously hurt Fmax.

changes.description: Expose a gated speculative RAS-pop pulse only when IF advances with an exact canonical return prediction.
Report whether a resolved canonical return was predicted so the RAS can retire committed pops without double-counting speculative debt.
Keep committed push/pop stack state, add a small pending speculative-pop counter for the IF-visible top, and clear speculative debt on redirect recovery.
Wire IF speculative-pop, EX predicted-pop retirement, and redirect recovery signals through the existing return_stack instance.
Add back-to-back return-chain and wrong-path predicted-return rollback coverage for the speculative RAS read pointer.

title: Factor EX control-flow constants
motivation: Prior bench micro-opts show that broader fetch prediction, register-file reshaping, RAS rewrites, and post-EX payload cleanup usually cost more timing than they save. The remaining attractive target is therefore a tiny local cleanup in the existing EX control-flow cone, where CoreMark branches and jumps already resolve and where no added state or bypassing is required.

hypothesis.motivation: Prior bench micro-opts show that broader fetch prediction, register-file reshaping, RAS rewrites, and post-EX payload cleanup usually cost more timing than they save. The remaining attractive target is therefore a tiny local cleanup in the existing EX control-flow cone, where CoreMark branches and jumps already resolve and where no added state or bypassing is required.
hypothesis.hypothesis: Factor the repeated `in.pc + 4` expressions in `rtl/ex_stage.sv` into one local `pc_plus4` wire and use it consistently for link write data, fall-through `pc_next`, RAS push addresses, and predicted-return fault recovery. At the same time, simplify branch/jump target misalignment checks to test only target bit 1, because RV32 branch/JAL immediates and JALR target formation already force bit 0 to zero. This should reduce duplicated constant-add and low-bit compare logic in the EX redirect/RVFI path without changing IPC, pipeline state, RAS policy, memory behavior, ALTOPS behavior, or the NRET=2 RVFI contract.

lesson: - 2026-05-02 hyp-20260502-001-r15s0 (regression, -36.56%): Forcing a shared EX `pc_plus4` net across redirect recovery, RAS push, jump link data, and RVFI `pc_next` can create a higher-fanout timing/routing point than duplicated local `in.pc + 4` adders, so tiny CSE-style cleanups in the control-flow cone are not necessarily FPGA wins.

title: Valid-bit front-end squashing
motivation:
The best bench core is now highly sensitive to small front-end and routing
changes. Prior structural attempts that added buffers, predictors, broad
bypasses, or register-file reshaping mostly regressed, so the next structural
target should remove existing hot-path logic rather than add new concurrency.

The current IF stage substitutes a 32-bit ADDI NOP whenever flush or redirect
is asserted so wrong-path instruction fields cannot trigger decode or
load-use hazards. That NOP mux sits directly on the imemData-to-decode path,
which is already a known fragile cone because this design has no true IF/ID
register.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Replace IF-stage NOP injection with valid-bit squashing. IF should always pass
the fetched instruction word through, but mark redirect, flush, and imem-stall
bubbles with valid=0. ID then treats any invalid input as a zeroed ID/EX
bubble before controls can reach EX, and the hazard unit gates its load-use
source comparisons with the IF/ID valid bit so raw wrong-path or unavailable
instruction fields cannot create spurious stalls.

This preserves the current one-instruction-per-cycle pipeline, redirect
priority, RVFI ordering, illegal-instruction trap discipline, and channel-1
tie-off. The expected win is a small Fmax improvement from removing the
32-bit NOP-select mux from the front-end decode/register-read cone, while
adding only a narrow valid gate off the main instruction data path.

changes.description: Stop replacing flushed or stalled instruction words with an ADDI NOP; always forward imem_data and rely on valid/prediction metadata to identify bubbles.
Capture a zeroed ID/EX bubble whenever the IF/ID valid bit is low, preserving reset/flush/stall priority so invalid decoded controls never reach EX.
Add an IF/ID valid input and gate load-use source comparisons so invalid raw instruction bits cannot create false stalls.
Wire the IF/ID valid bit into the hazard unit while keeping the top-level IO and RVFI NRET=2 contract unchanged.
Add or extend a redirect/imem-stall regression showing invalid IF bubbles do not redirect, retire, or trigger load-use stalls.

title: Countdown loop replay predictor
motivation:
Prior predictor attempts show that CoreMark's remaining control hazard cost is real,
but IF-side branch target adders, BTB/BHT table reads, PC tag compares, broader
JALR matches, and speculative RAS mutation have all lost more Fmax than they
saved. The current return-only predictor works because IF consumes tiny registered
state and exact instruction classification. A loop predictor should follow that
style by avoiding current-PC tag lookup entirely.

hypothesis.motivation:
Prior predictor attempts show that CoreMark's remaining control hazard cost is real,
but IF-side branch target adders, BTB/BHT table reads, PC tag compares, broader
JALR matches, and speculative RAS mutation have all lost more Fmax than they
saved. The current return-only predictor works because IF consumes tiny registered
state and exact instruction classification. A loop predictor should follow that
style by avoiding current-PC tag lookup entirely.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a one-entry backward-loop replay predictor trained only by legal, aligned,
taken conditional branches in EX. When EX redirects from a backward branch, store
the resolved target and the sequential span from target to branch PC; IF arms a
small countdown after the redirect and decrements it only on real sequential fetch
advances. When the countdown reaches the branch slot, IF predicts the stored target
instead of pc+4, with priority below EX redirects and exact RAS returns.

This recovers steady-state inner-loop back-edge bubbles without a branch immediate
adder, BTB/BHT read, or 32-bit PC tag compare on the fetch path; the IF critical
addition is a small counter-zero check and an extra mux input that reuses a
registered target. Carry pred_loop and pred_next_pc through ID/EX so EX suppresses
redirect only on an exact taken-branch hit and corrects all not-taken, alias,
nested-control-flow, or misaligned cases through the existing flush path. Because
prior general predictors regressed, keep it single-entry, conditional-branch-only,
and invalidate on intervening non-loop redirects rather than trying to handle every
control-flow form.

changes.description: Add a one-entry backward-loop countdown predictor with registered target/span state, EX training, redirect invalidation, and IF-side predicted-target output.
Extend IF/ID and ID/EX bundles with pred_loop metadata so EX can validate or repair countdown predictions without changing RVFI ports.
Consume the countdown predictor below RAS priority, update the countdown only when fetch advances, and emit pred_loop/pred_next_pc metadata.
Train the predictor from resolved taken backward conditional branches and suppress normal branch redirect only for exact loop-prediction hits.
Wire predictor train, invalidate, prediction, and metadata signals between IF and EX while preserving reset, stall, redirect, and RVFI behavior.
Add a small loop-prediction regression that checks a trained loop retires correctly and a not-taken loop exit redirects to the fall-through PC.
